zotero.abstractNote: Compositionality—the ability to combine known components to represent novel concepts—is a hallmark of human cognition. However, the computational costs of compositional processing are often overlooked. We investigate how time pressure affects the use of compositional strategies in a function learning task. Participants learned to predict outcomes based on combinations of features, where the underlying function was either compositional (additive) or non-compositional (configural). We find that under high time pressure, participants rely more on simple, non-compositional heuristics, whereas under low time pressure, they are more likely to employ compositional strategies. These results suggest that compositionality is a flexible, resource-dependent strategy that is modulated by the availability of cognitive resources.
